item.title: Young Hare
item.lesson: In 1502 nobody painted a rabbit just to paint a rabbit. Studies of animals existed as preparation for grander religious scenes, but Dürer signed and dated this watercolor with his famous 'AD' monogram as if it were a finished masterwork — because it is. Every layer of fur changes direction the way real fur does; the ears are alert at two different angles; the whiskers were pulled in single confident strokes. Dürer, the son of a Nuremberg goldsmith, fused German craftsmanship with ideas he absorbed on trips to Renaissance Italy, and he believed nature itself — not just scripture or mythology — deserved the artist's total attention. That conviction, radical in its quiet way, points forward to all of scientific illustration and every artist since who trusted that looking hard at an ordinary thing is enough.
item.lookFor: Zoom into the hare's eye: Dürer painted the reflection of a window there — the window of the studio where the animal sat, a room you can only visit inside that eye.
